g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we'll explore the fundamental building block of blockchain technology - the block structure.
g.text: The Structure of a Block in Blockchain

g.data-narration: A blockchain block has two main parts: the header containing metadata, and the body storing transaction data.
This diagram shows the complete block structure with header fields stacked above the transaction list.
The Version field is 4 bytes indicating which block validation rules to follow, allowing protocol upgrades.
g.text: Purpose: Indicates the block validation rules to follow

g.data-narration: The Previous Block Hash links blocks together. Any change to a previous block invalidates all subsequent blocks.
g.text: Purpose: Hash of previous block's header, creating the chain

g.data-narration: If an attacker modifies a past block, its hash changes, breaking the chain link and making tampering detectable.
g.text: If attacker modifies Block 100 → Hash changes → Block 101's "previous hash" mismatches →
Entire chain from Block 101 becomes INVALID!

g.data-narration: The Merkle Root is a hash of all transactions, computed using a binary tree structure for efficient verification.
g.text: Purpose: Hash of all transactions in the block (Merkle Tree)

g.data-narration: The Timestamp is a 4-byte Unix epoch time marking when the block was mined, used for difficulty adjustments.
g.text: Purpose: Unix timestamp when the block was mined

g.data-narration: The Difficulty Target (nBits) is a compact representation of the hash threshold required for a valid proof-of-work block.
g.text: Purpose: Target value that block hash must be less than for validity

g.data-narration: The Nonce is a 32-bit number that miners increment to find a valid hash below the target - the heart of proof-of-work.
g.text: Purpose: A 32-bit number miners increment to find a valid hash (Proof of Work)

g.data-narration: The Block Body contains the transaction counter followed by all transactions - the first being the special Coinbase transaction.
g.text: Contains: Transaction Counter + List of Transactions

g.data-narration: The Coinbase transaction is always first - it creates new bitcoins as miner reward with no inputs, only outputs.
g.text: • Always the FIRST transaction in block

g.data-narration: The block hash is calculated by double SHA-256 hashing only the 80-byte header - the body transactions are not hashed directly.
g.text: Block Hash = SHA256(SHA256(Block Header)) - Only header is hashed!

g.data-narration: Different blockchains have varying block sizes and times - Bitcoin targets 1MB/10min, Ethereum uses variable gas limits.
Orphan blocks occur when two miners find blocks simultaneously - the network eventually converges to the longest chain.
g.text: Valid blocks not part of main chain (temporary forks)

g.data-narration: Nodes validate blocks by checking header fields, transaction validity, Merkle root matches, and proof-of-work hash.
g.text: Version recognized | Prev hash exists | Timestamp reasonable |
Merkle root matches | All transactions valid |

g.data-narration: In summary: Block header has 6 fields (80 bytes), body contains transactions. Previous hash links blocks, Merkle root summarizes transactions, nonce enables mining.
